Question: Find $$\mathbf{\text{p}} \times \mathbf{\text{q}}$$ for $$\mathbf{\text{p}} = \langle 5 , 1 , 2 \rangle$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{q}} = \langle -2 , 0 , 1 \rangle .$$ Express the answer using standard unit vectors.

Suppose vectors $$\mathbf{\text{u}}$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{v}}$$ lie in the xy -plane (the z -component of each vector is zero). Now suppose the x - and y -components of $$\mathbf{\text{u}}$$ and the y -component of $$\mathbf{\text{v}}$$ are all positive, whereas the x -component of $$\mathbf{\text{v}}$$ is negative. Assuming the coordinate axes are oriented in the usual positions, in which direction does $$\mathbf{\text{u}} \times \mathbf{\text{v}}$$ point?
Use the properties of the cross product to calculate $$( \mathbf{\text{i}} \times \mathbf{\text{k}} ) \times ( \mathbf{\text{k}} \times \mathbf{\text{j}} ) .$$

Use Properties of the Cross Product to find the magnitude of $$\mathbf{\text{u}} \times \mathbf{\text{v}} ,$$ where $$\mathbf{\text{u}} = \langle -8 , 0 , 0 \rangle$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{v}} = \langle 0 , 2 , 0 \rangle .$$
Use determinant notation to find $$\mathbf{\text{a}} \times \mathbf{\text{b}} ,$$ where $$\mathbf{\text{a}} = \langle 8 , 2 , 3 \rangle$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{b}} = \langle -1 , 0 , 4 \rangle .$$
Find a unit vector orthogonal to both $$\mathbf{\text{a}}$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{b}} ,$$ where $$\mathbf{\text{a}} = \langle 4 , 0 , 3 \rangle$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{b}} = \langle 1 , 1 , 4 \rangle .$$
Find the area of the parallelogram $$P Q R S$$ with vertices $$P ( 1 , 1 , 0 ) , Q ( 7 , 1 , 0 ) , R ( 9 , 4 , 2 ) ,$$ and $$S ( 3 , 4 , 2 ) .$$

Calculate the triple scalar product $$\mathbf{\text{a}} \cdotp ( \mathbf{\text{b}} \times \mathbf{\text{c}} ) ,$$ where $$\mathbf{\text{a}} = \langle 2 , -4 , 1 \rangle ,$$ $$\mathbf{\text{b}} = \langle 0 , 3 , -1 \rangle ,$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{c}} = \langle 5 , -3 , 3 \rangle .$$

Find the volume of the parallelepiped formed by the vectors $$\mathbf{\text{a}} = 3 \mathbf{\text{i}} + 4 \mathbf{\text{j}} - \mathbf{\text{k}} ,$$ $$\mathbf{\text{b}} = 2 \mathbf{\text{i}} - \mathbf{\text{j}} - \mathbf{\text{k}} ,$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{c}} = 3 \mathbf{\text{j}} + \mathbf{\text{k}} .$$

Are the vectors $$\mathbf{\text{a}} = \mathbf{\text{i}} + \mathbf{\text{j}} - \mathbf{\text{k}} ,$$ $$\mathbf{\text{b}} = \mathbf{\text{i}} - \mathbf{\text{j}} + \mathbf{\text{k}} ,$$ and $$\mathbf{\text{c}} = \mathbf{\text{i}} + \mathbf{\text{j}} + \mathbf{\text{k}}$$ coplanar?
Answer: No, the triple scalar product is $$-4 \ne 0 ,$$ so the three vectors form the adjacent edges of a parallelepiped. They are not coplanar.

Question: Calculate the force required to produce $$15 \text{N} \cdotp \text{m}$$ torque at an angle of $$30 º$$ from a 150-cm rod.
